coredata.dc:title: Enzyme-mediated Ru@UiO-66@MnO2 NSs/thiamine-based ratiometric fluorescence sensor for visual detection of organophosphorus pesticide residues
coredata.dc:creator: Tong, Fei
Yang, Zan
Wang, Zheng
Liu, Wenya
Jiang, Wanqi
Zhu, Lu
Wang, Lei
Zheng, Mingming
Hou, Ruyan
Zhou, Yibin
Liu, Yingnan
coredata.dc:description: In view of the potential hazards of organophosphorus pesticides (OPs), this paper constructed a ratiometric fluorescent probe utilizing a functionalized metal–organic framework to detect OPs. Ru(bpy)3Cl2 was encapsulated inside UiO-66 as a reference signal, and MnO2 nanosheets (MnO2 NSs) were grown on the surface to obtain Ru@UiO-66@MnO2 NSs. Acetylcholinesterase catalyzed the decomposition of acetylcholine into reductive thiocholine, which consumed MnO2 NSs, thus restoring the Ru@UiO-66 fluorescence. Due to the enzymatic inhibition of OPs and the redox reaction between MnO2 NSs and thiamine, this probe emitted blue fluorescence in the presence of OPs. The probe achieved linear responses to dichlorvos and chlorpyrifos with LODs of 9.99 × 10-6 μg mL−1 and 9.99 × 10-5 μg mL−1. The probe exhibited a satisfactory recovery rate for OPs in green tea. Furthermore, a hydrogel detection platform was developed by embedding the probe into sodium alginate. Overall, this work provides a visual approach to detect OPs in agricultural products.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Metal–organic frameworks
Organophosphorus pesticides
Visual detection
Ratiometric fluorescence
Redox reaction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of the ratiometric fluorescent probe
Construction of the ratiometric fluorescent hydrogel
Detection of OPs in real samples
Construction of the ratiometric fluorescent probe
Determination of DDVP and CPF in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Anhui Ecological Fermentation Engineering Research Center for Functional Fruit Beverage
State Key Laboratory of Tea Plant Biology and Utilization
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: State Key Laboratory of Tea Plant Biology and Utilization

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Excellent Research and Innovation Team Project of Colleges and Universities in Anhui Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial supports of National Natural Science Youth Foundation of China (32202159), Natural Science Research Project of Colleges and Universities in Anhui Province (KJ2021A0142), the Open Fund of State Key Laboratory of Tea Plant Biology and Utilization (SKLTOF20220123), the Open Fund of Anhui Ecological Fermentation Engineering Research Center for Functional Fruit Beverage (Fuyang Normal University) (FSKFKT033D), and Excellent Research and Innovation Team Project of Colleges and Universities in Anhui Province (2022AH010055). The authors would like to thank the Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the assistance of XPS analysis.

The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial supports of National Natural Science Youth Foundation of China (32202159), Natural Science Research Project of Colleges and Universities in Anhui Province (KJ2021A0142), the Open Fund of State Key Laboratory of Tea Plant Biology and Utilization (SKLTOF20220123), the Open Fund of Anhui Ecological Fermentation Engineering Research Center for Functional Fruit Beverage (Fuyang Normal University) (FSKFKT033D), and Excellent Research and Innovation Team Project of Colleges and Universities in Anhui Province (2022AH010055). The authors would like to thank the Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the assistance of XPS analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the construction of the ratiometric fluorescent probe and the detection of OPs.

(A ∼ C) HR-TEM images of (A) UiO-66, (B) Ru@UiO-66, and (C) Ru@UiO-66@MnO2 NSs. The insets show the corresponding particle size distribution. (D) Elemental mapping of O, Zr, Ru, and Mn. (E) XRD spectra. (F) FT-IR spectra. (G) XPS survey spectrum of Ru@UiO-66@MnO2 NSs. (H, I) High-resolution XPS spectra of (H) N 1s and (I) Mn 2p. (J) ζ-potential. (K) TGA curves. (L) UV–vis absorption spectra. (M) Fluorescent spectra. (N, O) The influences of (N) pH and (O) UV irradiation on the fluorescence intensity of Ru@UiO-66@MnO2 NSs.

(A) Quenching efficiency of KMnO4 versus Ru@UiO-66 with different dosages. (B) Excitation-emission matrix spectra of Ru@UiO-66@MnO2 NSs/TH. (C) Fluorescence response of Ru@UiO-66@MnO2 NSs to different concentrations of TH. (D) Fluorescence response of Ru@UiO-66@MnO2 NSs to TH under different reaction times. (E-I) The effects of (E) ATCh concentration, (F) reaction time, (G) AChE concentration, (H) pH, and (I) reaction temperature of enzymatic hydrolysis on the fluorescence intensity ratio of the probe.

(A, D) Fluorescence emission spectra of the probe in presence of (A) DDVP and (D) CPF in a range of concentration gradients (DDVP: 0, 1 × 10-5, 5 × 10-5, 5 × 10-4, 5 × 10-3, 1 × 10-2, 0.1, 1, 2.5, 5, 8, 10 μg mL−1; CPF: 0, 1 × 10-4, 5 × 10-4, 1 × 10-3, 1 × 10-2, 0.1, 1, 2.5, 5, 8, 10, 12 μg mL−1). (B) Plot of fluorescence intensity ratio (I440/I625) versus logarithm of DDVP concentration. (E) Plot of fluorescence intensity ratio (I440/I625) versus CPF concentration. (C, F) The corresponding CIE chromaticity diagrams of (C) DDVP and (F) CPF.

(A) The fluorescence intensity ratio (I440/I625) of the probe in the presence of different interfering substances and (B) the corresponding fluorescent photo under 365 nm UV light. The concentrations of DDVP and CPF were 10 μg mL−1, while the concentrations of other interfering substances were 100 μg mL−1. (C) I440/I625 of the probe when OPs (20 μg mL−1) coexisted with other interfering substances (100 μg mL−1) and (D) the corresponding fluorescent photo.
(A, B) Photographs of hydrogel-based biosensors responding to (A) DDVP (0 ∼ 10 μg mL−1) and (B) CPF (0 ∼ 15 μg mL−1) under 365 nm UV light. Photographs were obtained from at least three replicates.

head.ce:title: Enzyme-mediated Ru@UiO-66@MnO2 NSs/thiamine-based ratiometric fluorescence sensor for visual detection of organophosphorus pesticide residues

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Jianghuai Agricultural Product Fine Processing and Resource Utilization of Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Affairs, State Key Laboratory of Tea Plant Biology and Utilization, Department of Food Science and Engineering, Anhui Agricultural University, Hefei 230036, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Jianghuai Agricultural Product Fine Processing and Resource Utilization of Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Affairs
State Key Laboratory of Tea Plant Biology and Utilization
Department of Food Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Jianghuai Agricultural Product Fine Processing and Resource Utilization of Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Affairs, State Key Laboratory of Tea Plant Biology and Utilization, Department of Food Science and Engineering, Anhui Agricultural University, Hefei 230036, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In view of the potential hazards of organophosphorus pesticides (OPs), this paper constructed a ratiometric fluorescent probe utilizing a functionalized metal–organic framework to detect OPs. Ru(bpy)3Cl2 was encapsulated inside UiO-66 as a reference signal, and MnO2 nanosheets (MnO2 NSs) were grown on the surface to obtain Ru@UiO-66@MnO2 NSs. Acetylcholinesterase catalyzed the decomposition of acetylcholine into reductive thiocholine, which consumed MnO2 NSs, thus restoring the Ru@UiO-66 fluorescence. Due to the enzymatic inhibition of OPs and the redox reaction between MnO2 NSs and thiamine, this probe emitted blue fluorescence in the presence of OPs. The probe achieved linear responses to dichlorvos and chlorpyrifos with LODs of 9.99 × 10-6 μg mL−1 and 9.99 × 10-5 μg mL−1. The probe exhibited a satisfactory recovery rate for OPs in green tea. Furthermore, a hydrogel detection platform was developed by embedding the probe into sodium alginate. Overall, this work provides a visual approach to detect OPs in agricultural products.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Tea is a popular beverage crop and plays an important role in people's diet. However, the high dependence on pesticides during tea growth and stable yield increases the risk of pesticide residues in tea (Miao, Wei, Pan, Wang, & Wei, 2023). Among various pesticides, organophosphorus pesticides (OPs) account for approximately 70%. However, due to abuse and improper treatment, OPs with high accumulation properties can easily remain in crops and transfer to the human body via the food chain (Liu et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2020). High doses of OPs can cause symptoms of acute intoxication, including cardiac arrhythmias, muscle twitching, and dyspnea. Low doses of OPs can also lead to chronic toxicity and damage to major organs, such as the liver and kidneys. Therefore, it is particularly important to realize simple, rapid, and sensitive detection of OPs residues in tea.

Fluorescence detection technology has become one of the most promising detection methods for OPs owing to its advantages of high sensitivity, convenient operation, and rapid response (Dong et al., 2020; Lin et al., 2018). Wang and coworkers developed a highly sensitive upconversion fluorescence probe that utilized enzymatic reactions and the energy transfer effect to respond to OPs in apple samples (Wang et al., 2019). Wu and coworkers developed a fluorescence sensing platform for OPs detection utilizing the aggregation of gold nanoparticles induced by the enzymatic reaction and the fluorescence resonance energy transfer effect between gold nanoparticles and carbon quantum dots (Wu et al., 2017). However, most reported fluorescent probes are based on a single detection signal, and the analytical performance is susceptible to interference by experimental factors unrelated to the target (Nair, Chandran, Mohamed, & Pillai, 2021; Yang, Li, Li, & Li, 2021). In contrast, ratiometric fluorescent probes utilize the ratio of two or more emission bands at different wavelengths as the analytical signal, which provides a self-calibration signal to eliminate the effects of probe concentration and environmental factors, thereby improving accuracy and sensitivity (Li et al., 2022; Liu et al., 2020). Nevertheless, to our knowledge, there are limited studies on ratiometric fluorescent probes for OPs detection.

As a typical representative MOF, UiO-66 can be used as a rigid/flexible host for luminescent guests due to its pole-regular channel structure and controllable pore size, thus generating guest-induced luminescence (Zhu et al., 2019). For example, Ruan et al. encapsulated sulfoyrodamine B into UiO-66 to prepare SRB@UiO-66 with excellent stability and realized the high-sensitivity detection of Fe3+ (Ruan et al., 2020). Feng and coworkers constructed an HCAA@UiO-66-based fluorescent probe for the sensitive response of Fe3+ by embedding 7-hydroxycoumarin-4-acetic acid (HCAA) molecules into the pore structure of UiO-66 (Feng et al., 2021). Compared with self-luminous MOFs, MOF-enclosing fluorophores can not only maintain the inherent structure of MOFs but also effectively prevent fluorescence quenching caused by MOF aggregation, thus improving the stability of fluorescence detection (Mayers & Larsen, 2020; Maza & Morris, 2014). In addition, the large specific surface area of UiO-66 is also good for the in situ growth of nanomaterials on the surface, and the internally enclosed guest fluorophore can be used as a fluorescence reference signal for ratiometric fluorescence analysis.

Inspired by the above facts, this study utilized UiO-66 to encapsulate Ru as a reference signal and in situ-grown manganese dioxide nanosheets (MnO2 NSs) on its surface to prepare nanocomposites (Ru@UiO-66@MnO2 NSs). A ratiometric fluorescent probe was elaborately constructed for the sensitive response of OPs utilizing the fluorescence quenching effect of MnO2 NSs, the oxidase enzyme-like activity, and the inhibition effect of OPs on the enzyme. As shown in Fig. 1 , Ru@UiO-66@MnO2 NSs exhibited weak red fluorescence due to the fluorescence quenching effect of MnO2 NSs. Acetylcholinesterase (AChE) catalyzes the hydrolysis of acetylthiocholine (ATCh) to a reductive thiocholine (TCh), which reduces MnO2 NSs to Mn2+, thereby restoring the red fluorescence of Ru@UiO-66. Due to the lack of oxidase-like activity, Mn2+ can not oxidize thiamine (TH), and the solution still emits red fluorescence. When OPs are present, the activity of AChE decreases, which hinders the generation of TCh and the decomposition of MnO2 NSs. The residual MnO2 NSs oxidizes TH into fluorescent thiochrome (TC), and the solution shows bright blue fluorescence. By adjusting the concentration of OPs, the probe solution can realize a color transition from red to blue that can be recognized by the naked eye. Furthermore, we also developed a fluorescent hydrogel detection platform by embedding the probe into the sodium alginate gel matrix, which provided a new method for portable and visual rapid detection of OPs.

Zirconyl chloride octahydrate (ZrOCl2), terephthalic acid (H2BDC), acetic acid, N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), tris-HCl buffer (2.0 mol/L, pH 7.5), 4-morpholineethanesulfonic acid (MES), Ru(bpy)3Cl2, histidine (l-His), cysteine (l-Cys), glucose (Glu), glucose oxidase (GoX), and TH were supplied by Macklin Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ethanol was obtained from Zhanyun Chemical Co. Ltd. (Wuxi, China). Potassium permanganate (KMnO4) was provided by Kelong Chemical Co. Ltd. (Chengdu, China). AChE and ATCh were obtained from Yuanye Biological Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). NaCl, KCl, MgCl2, Na2HPO4·12H2O, Na2CO3, Na2SO4, CaCl2, and NaH2PO4·2H2O were supplied by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Bovine serum albumin (BSA), thiamethoxam (THIA), metalaxyl (ETA), tebuconazole (TEBU), carbendazim (CARB), acetamiprid (ACET), prochloraz (PROCH), deltamethrin (DELTA), dichlorvos (DDVP), and chlorpyrifos (CPF) were supplied by Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All chemical reagents were of analytical grade and were used without further purification.

Morphologies of the prepared nanomaterials were characterized by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, FEI Talos F200X G2, USA). X-ray diffraction (XRD, SmartLab SE, Japan) patterns were used to characterize the crystal structure. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Nicolet 6700, USA) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha, USA) were performed to characterize the functional groups and elemental composition. The particle size and ζ-potential of the material were recorded by a Zeta potentiometer (Malvern, UK). Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA, TGA5500, Germany) was used to evaluate the thermal stability. Ultraviolet–visible spectra were measured by a UV-2600i spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fluorescence spectra were collected by a fluorescence spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer FL6500, USA). The slit widths for excitation and emission were both 10 nm. The initial concentration of OPs in actual samples was investigated by high-performance liquid chromatography (Shimadzu, Japan).

UiO-66 was synthesized according to a previous report with a slight change (Wang et al., 2017). In brief, H2BDC (1000 mg) and ZrClO2 (420 mg) were dissolved in 20 mL and 60 mL of DMF, respectively. Subsequently, the above solutions were mixed in a beaker containing 8 mL acetic acid, and the obtained mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave. After continuous heating at 90 °C for 18 h, the mixture was collected by centrifugation (14940 g, 15 min) and then washed with DMF and water. The resulting purified UiO-66 was dispersed in water for the following experiments.

Briefly, 25 mL of the prepared UiO-66 dispersion (5 mg mL−1) was mixed with Ru in DMF solution (25 mL, 1 mg mL−1). After 12 h of stirring at 90 °C, the final product was collected by centrifugation (14940 g, 15 min) and washed with DMF and ethanol. The yellow crystalline powder was obtained after vacuum drying at 60 °C for 12 h.

Ru@UiO-66@MnO2 NSs were prepared by an in situ growth method (Liu, Lei, Zhang, Wang, & He, 2021). First, 100 μL of Ru@UiO-66 solution (130 mg mL−1) was added to 250 μL of MES solution (0.1 mol/L, pH 6.0). Then, 100 μL of KMnO4 solution (2 ∼ 10 mmol/L) was added, and the resulting mixture was sonicated for 90 s. After standing for 30 min, the precipitate was collected by centrifugation (14940 g, 10 min) and purified by washing with deionized water. The obtained Ru@UiO-66@MnO2 NSs were dispersed in water and stored at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the ratiometric fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: The ratiometric fluorescent probe for OPs detection was constructed by mixing Ru@UiO-66@MnO2 NSs solution (5.5 mg mL−1, 250 μL) with TH solution (2.5 mmol/L, 150 μL). To obtain the optimal detection performance, key parameters, including excitation wavelength, KMnO4 concentration, TH concentration, reaction time, pH, ATCh concentration, and AChE concentration were optimized.

First, 20 μL of OPs, 380 μL of phosphate buffer solution (0.02 mol/L, pH 7), and 100 μL of AChE solution (100 mU mL−1) were mixed evenly and incubated at 37℃ for 30 min. Subsequently, ATCh solution (100 μL, 125 μmol/L) was introduced, and the mixture was reacted at 37 °C for 40 min. After that, 250 μL of Ru@UiO-66@MnO2 NSs (35 mg mL−1) was added. Two minutes later, 150 μL of TH solution (2.5 mmol/L) was injected into the above solution. After a further 40 min incubation at 37℃, the fluorescence emission spectra of the solution were collected (excitation wavelength: 370 nm, emission wavelength: 390 ∼ 700 nm).

The selectivity of the developed probe for OPs was evaluated. The experimental procedure was as described above, except that 20 μL of OPs (10 μg mL−1) was replaced by 20 μL of other potential interfering substances (100 μg mL−1). To cope with the complex detection environment, the anti-interference ability of this fluorescent probe to OPs was also investigated. A mixed solution of 10 μL of OPs (20 μg mL−1) and 10 μL of interfering substances (100 μg mL−1) was used to replace 20 μL of OPs solution in the above steps.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of the ratiometric fluorescent hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: Sodium alginate (0.1 g) was dissolved in 8 mL of Ru@UiO-66@MnO2 NSs solution. After stirring at room temperature for 2 h, 250 μL of the above mixture was injected into a specific mold, and then the mold was immersed in CaCl2 solution (0.07 mol/L) for 30 min. OPs, AChE, and ATCh were handled according to 2.7. The fluorescent hydrogel released from the mold was subsequently transferred to the above solution, and 150 μL of TH solution (2.5 mmol/L) was added after 2 min of incubation. After 2 h, the hydrogel was removed and photographed under 365 nm UV light.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of OPs in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The practical application potential of the probe in OPs detection was evaluated by the standard spiked recovery method. Green tea purchased from a local supermarket was selected as the research object. The initial OPs content in tea samples was detected on the basis of GB/T 23204–2008. Subsequently, equal amounts of tea samples were immersed in DDVP solution and CPF solution at concentrations of 0, 1, 2, and 4 μg mL−1. After the sample was air-dried, it was dispersed in 1 mL methanol. The resulting OPs extracts were treated according to 2.7.

Ru@UiO-66@MnO2 NSs were prepared in three steps. Briefly, UiO-66 was prepared via a facile solvothermal method with Zr4+ as the metal center and H2BDC as the ligand. After that, we used UiO-66 to encapsulate the fluorophore Ru for the preparation of Ru@UiO-66. MnO2 NSs were generated in situ at Ru@UiO-66 via a reduction reaction of KMnO4, and Ru@UiO-66@MnO2 NSs were obtained. The morphology was characterized by HR-TEM images. As shown in Fig. 2 A, UiO-66 had a uniform spherical structure with an average diameter of approximately 18 nm. After encapsulation of Ru, the microstructure of UiO-66 did not change distinctly (Fig. 2B), but the color of the powder changed from white to yellow. When MnO2 NSs were grown in situ on the surface of Ru@UiO-66, the morphology of Ru@UiO-66 became more regular and the particle size increased significantly (Fig. 2C). The dynamic light scattering result in Fig. S1 also confirmed the particle size change. The energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS)-elemental mappings (Fig. 2D) presented the uniform distribution of O, Zr, Ru, and Mn in Ru@UiO-66@MnO2 NSs. XRD patterns were used to reflect the crystal structure of the nanomaterial. As presented in Fig. 2E, UiO-66 exhibited three characteristic peaks at 7.3°, 8.4°, and 26°, corresponding to the (111), (200), and (531) crystal planes, respectively (Davoodian, Nakhaei Pour, Izadyar, Mohammadi, & Vahidi, 2021). After encapsulation of Ru and modification of MnO2 NSs, both Ru@UiO-66 and Ru@UiO-66@MnO2 NSs exhibited highly similar crystal structures, implying that the skeleton of UiO-66 was well preserved during functionalization.

FTIR spectra were recorded to characterize the functional groups. As shown in Fig. 2F, the FTIR spectra of UiO-66, Ru@UiO-66, and Ru@UiO-66@MnO2 NSs presented similar peaks. The asymmetric vibration of the Zr-O bond produced two typical vibration bands at 670 cm−1 and 740 cm−1 (Man et al., 2022). The peaks at 1420 cm−1 and 1580 cm−1 were derived from the symmetric vibration of the CC bond in the benzene ring and the stretching vibration of the carboxylic CO bond, respectively. This result indicated that the main functional groups in the BDC were preserved during the construction of Ru@UiO-66 and Ru@UiO-66@MnO2 NSs. Additionally, Ru@UiO-66 and Ru@UiO-66@MnO2 NSs showed characteristic peaks of Ru-derived pyridine groups at 1254 cm−1 and 1634 cm−1, indicating that Ru was successfully encapsulated into UiO-66 (Liu et al., 2020). Ru@UiO-66@MnO2 NSs also displayed a characteristic band of Mn-O vibration at 516 cm−1, suggesting that MnO2 NSs grew in situ on the surface of Ru@UiO-66 as expected.

XPS analysis was performed to reveal the chemical composition of the prepared Ru@UiO-66@MnO2 NSs. As shown in Fig. 2G, the characteristic peaks of Zr 3d (182.08 eV), C 1 s (284.08 eV), Ru 3d (284.08 eV), N 1 s (399.08 eV), O 1 s (531.08 eV), and Mn 2p (640.29 eV) appeared in the XPS survey spectrum. In Fig. 2H, the high-resolution XPS spectrum of N 1 s displayed two peaks located at 400.1 eV (NC) and 400.8 eV (NH), verifying the presence of a pyridine group derived from Ru. For the Mn 2p spectrum (Fig. 2I), the peak at 641.28 eV was ascribed to Mn 2p3/2. It revealed the presence of Mn4+ (641.18 eV) and further verified the formation of MnO2 NSs on the Ru@UiO-66 surface (Yuan et al., 2022). The ζ-potential results are presented in Fig. 2J. The ζ-potential of UiO-66 was 15.2 mV, and the ζ-potential increased to 29.6 mV after the encapsulation of Ru. After modification of MnO2 NSs, the potential of Ru@UiO-66 decreased to 9.4 mV, which was mainly caused by the negative potential of MnO2 NSs. These data provided strong support for the successful preparation of Ru@UiO-66@MnO2 NSs.

Thermogravimetric analysis was performed to reflect the thermal stability of the materials. As presented in Fig. 2K, the mass loss of Ru@UiO-66@MnO2 NSs was divided into three stages. Like most MOFs, the first weight loss below 100℃ was due to the evaporation of water and residual solvents. UiO-66 and Ru@UiO-66 exhibited significant weight loss at 100 ∼ 450℃, which may be caused by dehydroxylation of Zr-O. However, the loss of Ru@UiO-66@MnO2 NSs was slight, which may be due to the protective effect of MnO2 NSs on the UiO-66 skeleton. When the temperature rose to 580℃, the complete structural collapse of UiO-66 and the decomposition of organic ligands led to significant mass loss. The Ru@UiO-66@MnO2 NSs residue was 46.5%, which was significantly higher than that of the other two materials, indicating that Ru and Mn with good thermal stability were retained. This again verified the successful modification of Ru and MnO2 NSs.

The optical properties of Ru@UiO-66@MnO2 NSs were further analyzed. As shown in Fig. 2L, Ru displayed a characteristic peak at 453 nm. In contrast, the characteristic peak of Ru in Ru@UiO-66 was obviously wider and larger. After modification of MnO2 NSs, Ru@UiO-66@MnO2 NSs showed a wider absorption band at 350 ∼ 700 nm, which may be due to the wide characteristic absorption peak of MnO2 NSs at 350 ∼ 600 nm. Fig. 2M presents the fluorescence spectra of Ru@UiO-66@MnO2 NSs. MnO2 NSs modification significantly quenched the fluorescence peak at 625 nm originating from Ru based on the fluorescence resonance energy transfer effect or the inner filter effect. Correspondingly, Ru@UiO-66@MnO2 NSs emitted only faint red fluorescence under a 365 nm UV lamp. The photoluminescence stability of Ru@UiO-66@MnO2 NSs was analyzed by evaluating the effect of pH and UV irradiation on fluorescence intensity. As presented in Fig. 2N, the fluorescence intensity of Ru@UiO-66@MnO2 NSs was almost stable within a wide pH range (pH 5 ∼ 10), reflecting a weak protonation-deprotonation ability. The effect of photobleaching on fluorescence intensity is shown in Fig. 2O. The fluorescence intensity of Ru@UiO-66@MnO2 NSs only decreased by 4.2% after 100 min of continuous irradiation by a UV lamp, reflecting excellent photobleaching resistance. These data demonstrated that the prepared Ru@UiO-66@MnO2 NSs possessed excellent photoluminescence stability and had the potential to be used in fluorescence analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of the ratiometric fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: Many studies have reported that MnO2 NSs have oxidation-like activity, which can promote the oxidation of nonfluorescent TH into blue fluorescent TC (Hu, Liu, Zhang, Cao, & Huang, 2019; Hu, Fan, Jiang, Zhou, & Cheng, 2021; Xiao, Wang, Yan, Huang, & Yang, 2021; Zhang et al., 2021; Zhang, Gan, Zhen, Hu, & Hu, 2022). The fluorescence spectra in Fig. S2 also confirmed this. On this basis, a Ru@UiO-66@MnO2 NSs-based ratiometric fluorescent probe was constructed to detect OPs by utilizing the redox reaction between MnO2 NSs and TH, the fluorescence quenching effect of MnO2 NSs and the enzymatic reaction between AChE and ATCh. As presented in Fig. 1, if OPs were absent, AChE catalyzed the hydrolysis of ATCh to TCh, which reduced MnO2 NSs of Ru@UiO-66@MnO2 NSs to Mn2+. The solution emitted red fluorescence. The UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. S3A) and the fluorescence spectra (Fig. S3B) confirmed the occurrence of a redox reaction between TCh and MnO2 NSs. When OPs were present, the activity of AChE was inhibited, resulting in MnO2 NSs not being decomposed. The residual MnO2 NSs utilized oxidase-like properties to oxidize nonfluorescent TH to strongly fluorescent TC, while itself was reduced to Mn2+. The fluorescence spectra in Fig. S3C show that Ru@UiO-66@MnO2 NSs exhibited a strong fluorescence signal at approximately 440 nm after reaction with TH, and the solution emitted blue fluorescence under a 365 nm UV lamp. This confirmed the existence of a redox reaction between TH and MnO2 NSs. Furthermore, different concentrations of enzymatic reaction products were introduced into the Ru@UiO-66@MnO2 NSs/TH system. As shown in Fig. S3D, the intensity of the fluorescence originating from TC at 440 nm significantly decreased with increasing AChE concentration, which verified the regulatory effect of the enzyme reaction on the fluorescence signal. Therefore, the inhibitory effect of OPs on enzyme activity could be utilized to regulate the fluorescence signal of the probe to achieve a sensitive response to OPs.

To obtain optimal detection performance, various key parameters include the content of MnO2 NSs in Ru@UiO-66@MnO2 NSs, excitation wavelength, TH concentration, incubation time, reaction temperature, pH, ATCh concentration, and enzyme concentration. Fig. 3 A presents the quenching efficiency of KMnO4 concentration on the fluorescence intensity of Ru@UiO-66 during the in situ reduction of MnO2 NSs. With the increasing concentration of KMnO4, more MnO2 NSs grew on the Ru@UiO-66 surface, which quenched the fluorescence of Ru@UiO-66 through the fluorescence resonance energy transfer effect or the inner filter effect. The fluorescence quenching efficiency reached more than 70% and no longer increased when the concentration of KMnO4 exceeded 6 mmol/L. Therefore, a KMnO4 concentration of 6 mmol/L was chosen for the preparation of Ru@UiO-66@MnO2 NSs.

Fig. 3B illustrates the emission spectra of the Ru@UiO-66@MnO2 NSs/TH-based fluorescent probe at different excitation wavelengths. At an excitation wavelength of 370 nm, the fluorescence intensity derived from TC at 440 nm was the highest, while the fluorescence intensity from Ru at 625 nm had no significant change. Thus, 370 nm was chosen as the optimal excitation wavelength for fluorescence analysis in subsequent experiments.

The concentration of TH was closely related to the intensity of the blue fluorescence peak. As displayed in Fig. 3C, I440/I625 increased with increasing TH concentration, indicating that more TH was oxidized to blue fluorescent TC. However, I440/I625 tended to balance when the TH concentration exceeded 2.5 mmol/L. This may be due to the 2.5 mmol/L TH completely decomposing the MnO2 NSs on the surface of Ru@UiO-66@MnO2 NSs. Therefore, the optimum concentration of TH was set to 2.5 mmol/L. The reaction time between Ru@UiO-66@MnO2 NSs and TH was also optimized. Fig. 3D shows that I440/I625 basically remained constant after the reaction time exceeded 40 min, suggesting that the reaction between Ru@UiO-66@MnO2 NSs and TH reached a dynamic equilibrium within 40 min.

Additionally, the influence of the factors related to the enzymatic reaction on the fluorescence signal was also explored. OPs inhibited the activity of AChE and prevented ATCh from being hydrolyzed to a reductive TCh. The residual Ru@UiO-66@MnO2 NSs emitted dual fluorescence signals via oxidizing TH. Thus, the concentration of ATCh was closely related to the value of I440/I625, and the relationship further served as a bridge between OPs and I440/I625. In Fig. 3E, when the ATCh concentration reached 125 μmol/L, I440/I625 no longer decreased and tended to be stable. This was mainly because the reaction between AChE and ATCh was nearly complete and the resulting reducing TCh decomposed MnO2 NSs, thereby preventing TH oxidation and restoring the fluorescence of Ru. Similarly, the AChE concentration also affected the I440/I625 ratio. As shown in Fig. 3G, I440/I625 tended to be constant when AChE concentration exceeded 100 mU mL−1, indicating that ATCh was completely consumed by AChE. It further reflected that the fluorescence intensity ratio (I440/I625) of the probe reached the lowest when OPs was not present. Thus, the optimal concentration of AChE was set as 100 mU mL−1. The effect of the enzymatic reaction time was presented in Fig. 3F. The enzymatic reaction reached equilibrium within 40 min.

pH is an important factor affecting the fluorescence intensity and stability of nanomaterials. As displayed in Fig. 3H, neutral and weak alkalinity (pH = 7 ∼ 9) favored the reaction between TH and Ru@UiO-66@MnO2 NSs. Under these conditions, AChE had higher enzyme activity and catalyzed the decomposition of ATCh into TCh, thus reducing MnO2 NSs on Ru@UiO-66@MnO2 NSs to Mn2+. Mn2+ cannot oxidize TH, resulting in a significant decrease in I440/I625 value. In the presence of OPs, the enzyme activity of AChE was inhibited and a series of subsequent reactions were hindered, leading to a significant increase in I440/I625 value. Since the fluorescence response of the probe was most significant at pH = 7, pH = 7 was selected for subsequent experiments. Fig. 3I presents the influence of incubation temperature on the fluorescence signal. I440/I625 changed little in the range of 4 ∼ 45℃, but it increased remarkably when the incubation temperature exceeded 45℃. This was mainly caused by the inactivation of AChE leading to residual MnO2 NSs.

Based on the above data, the optimal construction and detection conditions of this probe included the following: the concentration of KMnO4 was 6 mmol/L, the excitation wavelength was 370 nm, the concentration of TH was 2.5 mmol/L, the reaction time of Ru@UiO-66@MnO2 NSs and TH was 40 min, the concentration of ATCh was 125 μmol/L, the enzymatic reaction time was 40 min, the concentration of AChE was 100 mU mL−1, the pH of the reaction system was pH 7, and the incubation temperature was 37℃.

Under the optimal conditions, DDVP and CPF were used as representative OPs to explore the sensitivity of the developed Ru@UiO-66@MnO2 NSs/TH-based ratiometric fluorescent probe. As presented in Fig. 4 A, with increasing DDVP concentration, the fluorescence peak at 440 nm originating from TC markedly increased, while the fluorescence peak at 625 nm belonging to Ru remained basically unchanged. The logarithm of DDVP concentration linearly correlated with the fluorescence intensity ratio (I440/I625) in the range of 1 × 10-5 ∼ 1 × 10-2 μg mL−1 and 0.1 ∼ 8 μg mL−1 (Fig. 4B). The regression equations were expressed as y = 0.106lgx + 1.364 (R2 = 0.990) and y = 1.025lgx-2.844 (R2 = 0.998). The limit of detection (LOD) calculated according to the 3d/s rule (d is the standard deviation of the blank, and s denotes the slope of the standard curve) was 9.99 × 10-6 μg mL−1. The inset in Fig. 4A displays a fluorescent color transition from red to blue in the probe solution with increasing DDVP concentration under 365 nm UV light. The International Commission on Illumination (CIE) chromaticity (Fig. 4C) further provided strong support for these color variations.

The probe also displayed a similar fluorescence response to CPF. In Fig. 4D, the intensity variation trend of the two fluorescence peaks was similar to that of DDVP. The CPF concentration was linearly correlated with the fluorescence intensity ratio (I440/I625) at 1 × 10-4 ∼ 10 μg mL−1 (Fig. 4E). The corresponding linear equation was y = 0.179x + 1.290 (R2 = 0.993). The LOD was calculated to be 9.99 × 10-5 μg mL−1. An obvious fluorescent color transition could be observed in the illustration of Fig. 4D and in Fig. 4F.

Compared with other reported fluorescent sensors in Table S1, the proposed ratiometric fluorescent sensor displayed lower detection limits and a wider detection range (Chen et al., 2019; Chen, Chen, Wang, Liu, & Chi, 2021; Eskandari, Amirzehni, Hassanzadeh, & Vahid, 2020; Jin et al., 2019; Li et al., 2020; Lin et al., 2021; Ma et al., 2020; Wu et al., 2017; Yao et al., 2019). Thus, the probe based on Ru@UiO-66@MnO2 NSs/TH can achieve sensitive detection of OPs.

Considering the complexity of real samples, we further analyzed the selectivity of the probe. As shown in Fig. 5 A, only DDVP and CPF caused significant enhancement of the fluorescence intensity ratio. Other interfering substances, including Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, CO3 2–, SO4 2-, HPO4 2-, Glu, l-His, l-Cys, GoX, BSA, THIA, ETA, TEBU, CARB, ACET, PROCH and DELTA, had only a slight effect on I440/I625. The fluorescence photos in Fig. 5B also indicated that interfering substances other than DDVP and CPF did not change the fluorescence color of the probe solution. To eliminate the possible fluorescent interference caused by the tested pesticides, the fluorescence emission spectra of nine pesticides were collected. As shown in Fig. S4, these pesticides had little intrinsic fluorescence signal and produced little fluorescence interference even when they coexisted with Ru@UiO-66@MnO2 NSs/TH. Therefore, the change of probe fluorescence signal in Fig. 5A was due to the inhibition effect of DDVP and CPF on AChE rather than the fluorescence interference caused by pesticides themselves. These results indicated that the developed ratiometric fluorescent probe possessed excellent selectivity for OPs. The detection stability of the probe was further explored when interfering substances and OPs coexisted. As shown in Fig. 5C, even if these interfering substances coexisted with OPs at 5 times the OPs concentration, there was little interference with the detection performance. The fluorescent image at 365 nm UV light also confirmed this phenomenon (Fig. 5D). Therefore, the developed fluorescence detection platform had excellent anti-interference performance and was expected to be applied to actual sample OPs detection.

To achieve better portable detection, Ru@UiO-66@MnO2 NSs were embedded in a sodium alginate hydrogel matrix to develop a batch of hydrogels for fluorescence detection of OPs. The blue fluorescence of the hydrogel was significantly enhanced with increasing DDVP concentration in the range of 0 ∼ 10 μg mL−1. Visual color transformation from red to blue can be achieved under a 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. 6 A). The hydrogel also showed similar detection performance for CPF at 0 ∼ 15 μg mL−1 (Fig. 6B). This result indicated that the developed fluorescent hydrogel has the potential to achieve portable visual detection of OPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of DDVP and CPF in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In view of the high OPs residual risk in tea, we verified the practical application potential of the developed Ru@UiO-66@MnO2 NSs/TH based ratiometric fluorescent sensor in detecting OPs with green tea as a real sample. As presented in Table S2, the recoveries of DDVP and CPF were 86.3 ∼ 103.4% and 86.7 ∼ 101.7%, respectively. The relative standard deviations (RSD) were lower than 3%, demonstrating excellent accuracy and stability. Taken together, the proposed probe can be used for the sensitive and accurate detection of OPs in tea sample, and it also has broad application prospects in other crops and their products.

In summary, we successfully constructed a Ru@UiO-66@MnO2 NSs-based ratiometric fluorescent biosensor for the highly sensitive and visual detection of OPs. In this strategy, the reaction products of Ru@UiO-66@MnO2 NSs and TH were used as fluorescent response signals, and the highly stable Ru@UiO-66 was used as the reference signal. OPs can regulate the output of fluorescence signals by inhibiting the activity of enzymes. The developed sensor can realize a sensitive response to DDVP and CPF, and the detection limits were as low as 9.99 × 10-6 μg mL−1 and 9.99 × 10-5 μg mL−1, respectively. Meanwhile, the response of the probe to the OPs concentration can be easily identified by the naked eye under a 365 nm UV lamp. The sensor has been successfully applied to the monitoring of OPs in tea with high accuracy and recovery. Furthermore, a novel hydrogel device was successfully fabricated for the visual detection of DDVP and CPF under a 365 nm UV lamp. This study not only provides a feasible strategy for the visual detection of OPs residues by ratiometric fluorescence but also illustrates a new method for the identification of other hazard factors.

Fei Tong: Writing – original draft. Zan Yang: Methodology. Zheng Wang: Investigation. Wenya Liu: Data curation. Wanqi Jiang: Software. Lu Zhu: Validation. Lei Wang: Formal analysis. Mingming Zheng: Conceptualization. Ruyan Hou: Conceptualization. Yibin Zhou: Conceptualization. Yingnan Liu: Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial supports of National Natural Science Youth Foundation of China (32202159), Natural Science Research Project of Colleges and Universities in Anhui Province (KJ2021A0142), the Open Fund of State Key Laboratory of Tea Plant Biology and Utilization (SKLTOF20220123), the Open Fund of Anhui Ecological Fermentation Engineering Research Center for Functional Fruit Beverage (Fuyang Normal University) (FSKFKT033D), and Excellent Research and Innovation Team Project of Colleges and Universities in Anhui Province (2022AH010055). The authors would like to thank the Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the assistance of XPS analysis.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2023.136945.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
